title: Inflation and unemployment rate in the UK
subtitle: Unit 15 'Inflation, unemployment, and monetary policy’ Section 15.7 'Supply shocks and inflation' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at: https://tinyco.re/15071330 [Figure 15.13]
caption:
Inflation and unemployment rate in the UK

Unit 15 'Inflation, unemployment, and monetary policy’ Section 15.7 'Supply shocks and inflation' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at: https://tinyco.re/15071330 [Figure 15.13]

This line chart plots the relationship between the inflation rate and the unemployment rate in the United Kingdom from 1989 to 2020. The vertical axis represents the inflation rate in percent, ranging from 0% to 8%, while the horizontal axis displays the unemployment rate in percent, spanning from 4% to 11%. A single red line traces the annual data points, with specific years labeled to mark positions along the trajectory. The path begins in 1989 with an unemployment rate near 7% and an inflation rate just below 6%, rising to a peak inflation level of approximately 8% in 1990 while unemployment remained steady. Following this peak, the line descends sharply in inflation while moving rightward to higher unemployment, reaching roughly 10.5% unemployment and roughly 2.5% inflation by 1993. The trajectory then loops leftward through the late 1990s and 2000s, generally staying below 4% inflation and fluctuating between 4% and 6% unemployment. Notable points include a dip to roughly 0.5% inflation around 2000 and a rise to nearly 4% unemployment in 2008. In 2011, the line spikes upward to nearly 4% inflation with unemployment around 8%, before dropping back down. The most recent labeled years, 2017 and 2018, sit at the far left of the chart, indicating unemployment rates between 4% and 5% with inflation hovering between 2% and 3%.
